schools.summary: Gill: Korah was a grandson of Kohath, cousin to Moses and Aaron, named first as the contriver and ringleader of the sedition, called 'the gainsaying of Korah' (Jude 11); he joined the Reubenites Dathan and Abiram.

themes: Korah... and Dathan and Abiram... and On... took men
priestly envy and tribal ambition united
the gainsaying of Korah (Jude 11)

schools.summary: Gill: they rose up before Moses to his face, openly and boldly, with impudence.
Keil & Delitzsch: two hundred fifty princes of the assembly, famous in the congregation, men of renown, joined them.

themes: two hundred and fifty princes of the assembly, famous... men of renown
the honoured leading the revolt
rank no proof of being right

schools.summary: Gill: they gathered together against Moses and Aaron, charging them with taking too much upon themselves.

Clarke: the spirit of the saying — 'holy offices are not equally distributed; you arrogate the most important as if your superior holiness entitled you to them,' pleading the holiness of the whole congregation.

themes: Ye take too much upon you, seeing all the congregation are holy
a half-truth made a pretext
ambition cloaked as zeal for equality

schools.summary: Gill: he heard their complaint against him for setting up Aaron, and against Aaron for taking the honour.
Keil & Delitzsch: he fell upon his face to complain of the matter to the Lord, as in ch. 14:5.

themes: when Moses heard it, he fell upon his face
prayer his first act, not self-defence
the meek man leaving his cause to God (Num. 12:3)

schools.summary: Gill: he spake this after he had finished his prayer and received instruction from God, referring the matter to him to decide who was holy and chosen.
Keil & Delitzsch: tomorrow Jehovah would show who was his and holy, and cause him to come near.

themes: to morrow the LORD will shew who are his, and who is holy
the dispute referred to God's decision
the Lord knoweth them that are his (2 Tim. 2:19)

schools.summary: Gill: the censers were vessels to put incense in to offer, which was the business of the priests.

Ellicott: the offering of incense was the peculiar prerogative and holiest function of the priesthood; the destruction of Nadab and Abihu ought to have warned Korah's company not to provoke a similar judgment.

themes: Take you censers, Korah, and all his company
the priest's holiest function
the claimants proved by the very thing they claimed

schools.summary: Gill: put fire and incense before the LORD, and the man whom the LORD chooses shall be holy.
Keil & Delitzsch: Moses retorts their own word — 'ye take too much upon you, ye sons of Levi' — the decision left to God's choice.

themes: the man whom the LORD doth choose, he shall be holy
their own charge turned back on them
the dispute settled by God's own choice

schools.summary: Gill: Moses continued his discourse to Korah as the head of the conspiracy and ringleader. Calvin: he addresses the whole body though speaking to Korah, who had corrupted the other Levites and is first summoned to God's tribunal.

themes: Moses said unto Korah, Hear, I pray you, ye sons of Levi
reasoning before judgment
a shepherd's patience with men set on ruin

schools.summary: Gill: it was a great thing God had done for them, with which they should have been satisfied and thankful.
Wesley: to minister to the congregation made them servants of both God and the church — a high dignity, though not sufficient for their ambitious minds.

themes: Seemeth it but a small thing... that the God of Israel hath separated you... to bring you near to himself
ingratitude for a real privilege
discontent the seed of rebellion

schools.summary: Gill: Korah is personally addressed — God had brought him near, and yet he sought the priesthood also.
Ellicott: the Targums understand the reference to the high priesthood, which Korah may have aimed at.

themes: he hath brought thee near to him... and seek ye the priesthood also?
ambition grasping the office not given
no man takes this honour uncalled (Heb. 5:4)

schools.summary: Gill: to gather against God's ministers, whom he had put into office, and to resist his ordinance, is interpreted as gathering against God; 'what is Aaron?' — he was but God's appointed servant, the quarrel really with God.

themes: both thou and all thy company are gathered together against the LORD
Aaron but God's appointed servant
resistance to God's order a quarrel with God

schools.summary: Gill: Moses sent to call them to the court of judicature; they had departed thinking the matter concerned only Korah and the Levites, and now flatly refused to come, in open contempt.

themes: Moses sent to call Dathan and Abiram... which said, We will not come up
rebellion bared of all pretext
the contempt that will not submit to a hearing

schools.summary: Gill: they meant Egypt, which never deserved this epithet, peculiar to Canaan; their applying it to Egypt argued great impudence and want of reverence for God, accusing Moses of making himself a prince to kill them in the wilderness.

themes: Is it a small thing that thou hast brought us up out of a land that floweth with milk and honey...?
Egypt styled with the phrase reserved for Canaan
rebellion inverting the truth

schools.summary: Gill: this was no fault of Moses, who had brought them to the borders and bid them go up; the people refused, listened to the spies, and provoked God to turn them back; 'put out the eyes' means, will you blind or deceive us?

themes: thou hast not brought us into a land that floweth with milk and honey... we will not come up
blaming Moses for their own forfeited land
rebellion never owning its fault

schools.summary: Gill: Moses burned with wrath, not so much at their ill usage of him as for the dishonour cast upon the Lord; he had taken nothing from them and hurt none.
Clarke: there was no danger God would respect their offering, for they wished to set up a priesthood of their own, which God never blessed.

themes: Moses was very wroth... Respect not thou their offering: I have not taken one ass from them
holy anger for God's dishonour
the clear conscience's answer to slander

schools.summary: Gill: Korah was still with Moses when the messenger returned from Dathan and Abiram.
Keil & Delitzsch: he summoned Korah and his associates to present themselves before Jehovah with censers and incense on the morrow.

themes: Be thou and all thy company before the LORD, thou, and they, and Aaron, to morrow
both sides to stand before God
the issue settled by God's visible verdict

schools.summary: Gill: the censers might be ones they had used in their families before the priesthood was limited to Aaron's line, or chafing-dishes serving the present purpose; all alike were to put fire and incense and stand before the LORD.

themes: take every man his censer... two hundred and fifty censers; thou also, and Aaron
all on equal terms before God
the verdict left wholly to God

schools.summary: Gill: they came prepared with censers and incense, taking fire from off the altar of burnt offering. Calvin: how greatly they were blinded by pride, that despite the confidence of Moses and the previous examples they still obstinately persisted.

themes: they took every man his censer... and stood in the door of the tabernacle
the blindness of pride persisting
walking open-eyed into judgment

schools.summary: Gill: Korah gathered not his own company only but as many of the congregation as he could, who seemed generally inclined to take his part; and the glory of the LORD appeared to all the congregation.

themes: Korah gathered all the congregation against them... and the glory of the LORD appeared
the people drawn to the rebel side
God's majesty answering the multitude

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake out of the cloud to Moses and Aaron, against whom the whole congregation had gathered.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron
God speaking at the height of the crisis
taking up the cause of his beset servants

schools.summary: Gill: the command was to separate not only from Korah's company but from the congregation, whom Korah had gathered and who by standing with him showed themselves on his side, greatly provoking the LORD.
Ellicott: separate yourselves from among the congregation, that God might consume them in a moment.

themes: Separate yourselves from among this congregation, that I may consume them in a moment
the people implicated by siding with Korah
to stand with rebels is to share their peril

schools.summary: Gill: they fell on their faces to deprecate God's wrath and avert the threatened judgment.
Clarke: 'O God, the God of the spirits of all flesh' proves they believed man a being compounded of body and an immortal spirit, God the Maker of all souls.

themes: O God, the God of the spirits of all flesh, shall one man sin, and wilt thou be wroth with all?
interceding to the Maker of every soul
the guilt of the few not to ruin the many

schools.summary: Gill: God bid Moses, on his face in prayer, rise up, having granted his request, and then spoke to him.
Henry: here is the determining of the controversy with Dathan and Abiram, as the next with Korah and his company.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
God answering the intercession
prayer prevailing to spare the many

schools.summary: Gill: this was spoken to the people of Israel of the several tribes assembled, not to Korah's company.
Keil & Delitzsch: get you up from about the tabernacle of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.

themes: Get you up from about the tabernacle of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram
a last mercy to the people
come out from the rebellious lest you share their doom (2 Cor. 6:17)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses arose and went about the divine direction directly.
Wesley: he went unto Dathan and Abiram because they had refused to come to him.

themes: Moses rose up and went unto Dathan and Abiram; and the elders of Israel followed him
going to those who refused to come
a last warning before the judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the congregation were assembled, some inclining to Korah's side out of ill will, some out of curiosity; they were to depart from the tents and touch nothing of theirs, lest they share their judgment.

themes: Depart... from the tents of these wicked men, and touch nothing of theirs
entire separation from the rebellious
lest you partake of their plagues (Rev. 18:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the place was encompassed by people from the several tribes who wished them well or were curious how it would issue.
Ellicott: Dathan and Abiram came out and stood in the door of their tents with their wives, sons, and little children.

themes: Dathan and Abiram came out, and stood in the door of their tents, and their wives... and their little children
the hardened rebels braving it out
rebellion ruining those bound to the rebel

schools.summary: Gill: the works in question — bringing Israel out of Egypt, the Levites for the firstborn, Aaron's priesthood — were the very things the rebels found fault with; Moses declares he did none of them of his own mind.
Keil & Delitzsch: hereby they should know that the LORD had sent him to do all these works.

themes: Hereby ye shall know that the LORD hath sent me... I have not done them of mine own mind
answering the charge of self-will
vindication put on God's visible act

schools.summary: Gill: 'the common death of all men' is 'as every man dies,' the death by disease or age that befalls the generality; Moses puts his own commission to the proof, staking it on the manner of their death.

themes: If these men die the common death of all men... then the LORD hath not sent me
the staked commission
a boldness measuring his certainty

schools.summary: Gill: 'make a new thing' is 'create a creation,' a death none ever died before.
Clarke: 'if Jehovah should create a creation' — do such a thing as was never done before — and they go down alive into the pit, proving they provoked the LORD.

themes: if the LORD make a new thing, and the earth open her mouth, and swallow them up
a judgment without precedent
the unprecedented stroke proving their guilt

schools.summary: Gill: as soon as he had finished his discourse to the congregation, immediately so it was — the ground clave asunder.

themes: as he had made an end of speaking all these words... the ground clave asunder
the word and the wonder meeting exactly
God setting his seal to his servant

schools.summary: Gill: the earth, as if a living creature, opened its mouth and swallowed Dathan and Abiram, their wives, sons, little ones, and goods.
Keil & Delitzsch: the earth opened her mouth and swallowed them up and their houses.

themes: the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up, and their houses... and all their goods
the ground gaping like a beast of prey
rebellion blotted out entirely (Ps. 106:17)

schools.summary: Gill: they and their families went down alive into the grave the opening earth made for them.
Clarke: the earthquake swallowed Korah's company alive into the pit, while the fire from the LORD consumed the 250 men that bare censers.

themes: They, and all that appertained to them, went down alive into the pit
taken living into the grave, the earth closing over them
cut off so utterly their place knows them no more

schools.summary: Gill: their cry was so loud, their shrieks so dreadful and piercing, that the Israelites fled to get out of the sound of them and for their own safety, lest the earth swallow them up also.

themes: all Israel that were round about them fled at the cry of them
fearing to be swallowed too
a wholesome terror at God's judgment on rebellion

schools.summary: Gill: it was flashes of lightning from the cloud in which God was.
Keil & Delitzsch: the 250, probably still before the tabernacle, were destroyed by fire from Jehovah, as Nadab and Abihu had been (Lev. 10:2).

themes: there came out a fire from the LORD, and consumed the two hundred and fifty men that offered incense
the stroke that fell on Nadab and Abihu (Lev. 10:2)
the presumptuous consumed by the holiness they handled

schools.summary: Gill: God spoke immediately after the men were consumed, out of the same cloud from whence the fire proceeded.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD commanded that Eleazar take up the censers from the midst of the burning.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
directing what should be done with the censers
the instruments of rebellion turned to a lasting witness

schools.summary: Gill: Eleazar, not Aaron, was bidden to do it, partly because Aaron was officiating and must not be defiled with the dead, partly as more fitting for the son; the censers, brought before the LORD, were now hallowed.

themes: that he take up the censers out of the burning... for they are hallowed
the misused vessel retaining a sacredness
the high priest's purity guarded

schools.summary: Gill: these were sinners against their own souls, who by burning incense sinned and ruined themselves.
Ellicott: 'sinners against their own souls' are men who forfeited their lives by their sin; the sacrilege was made the occasion of a permanent warning against all similar profanation.

themes: The censers of these sinners against their own souls, let them make them broad plates for a covering of the altar
the metal of presumption put to holy use
a standing sign against profanation

schools.summary: Gill: the metal is named — brass — fit for covering the altar of burnt offering, which was overlaid with brass; by this it appears these censers differed from Aaron's, which were gold.

themes: Eleazar the priest took the brasen censers... made broad plates for a covering of the altar
the baser metal marking them no true priests
even these devoted to a holy end

schools.summary: Gill: it was to keep in mind that none but Aaron's seed should come near to offer incense. Calvin: this confirms that the priesthood was God's appointment.
Ellicott: the apostle Jude warns Christians by this same example against the profanation of divine ordinances (Jude 11).

themes: that no stranger, which is not of the seed of Aaron, come near to offer incense... that he be not as Korah
the priesthood God's appointment, not man's ambition
the gainsaying of Korah a type for the church (Jude 11)

schools.summary: Gill: this was the day after the earth swallowed Dathan and Abiram and the fire consumed the 250.
Henry: a new rebellion raised the very next day — 'Be astonished, O heavens, at this' — an instance of the incurable corruption of sinners, calling the rebels 'the people of the LORD.'

themes: on the morrow all the congregation... murmured... Ye have killed the people of the LORD
a new rebellion the very next day
the incurable corruption of sinners

schools.summary: Gill: they gathered against Moses and Aaron to kill them, showing by their gestures an intention to murder them.
Keil & Delitzsch: when the congregation was gathered, they looked toward the tabernacle and the cloud covered it, and the glory of the LORD appeared.

themes: the congregation was gathered against Moses and... Aaron... the glory of the LORD appeared
the murderous mob confronted
God's majesty rescuing the faithful

schools.summary: Gill: they came from their tent to the tabernacle, both for shelter and safety and for advice and instruction how to behave in this crisis, standing in the door, the LORD being in the cloud over it.

themes: Moses and Aaron came before the tabernacle of the congregation
fleeing to the presence of God
seeking both refuge and direction

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake out of the cloud.
Keil & Delitzsch: vv.44-46, God bid them get up that he might consume the congregation, and Moses sent Aaron with the censer.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
God speaking again at the height of danger
his voice the stay of the faithful

schools.summary: Gill: the command to withdraw and be separate, that they not be involved in the destruction, God seeming to bid them let him alone to destroy the people. Calvin: God regards his people with such favour that he seems unwilling to strike while his servants plead.

themes: Get you up from among this congregation, that I may consume them... And they fell upon their faces
the mediators interceding again
God ever willing to be entreated

schools.summary: Gill: the censer lay in the tabernacle; Aaron was to take it with fire from the altar and incense, and make atonement, for the plague was begun.
Clarke: God now punished by a secret blast, his hand alone seen both in the plague and in the manner it was arrested.

themes: Take a censer... make an atonement for them: for there is wrath gone out from the LORD; the plague is begun
the appointed remedy atonement, not flight
the despised priesthood becoming the people's deliverance

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron took the censer with fire and incense and ran into the midst of the congregation, the plague begun, and made atonement.
Ellicott: the occasion was extraordinary, for ordinarily incense might be offered only on the golden altar within the holy place.

themes: Aaron... ran into the midst of the congregation... and made an atonement for the people
the priest running toward the wrath
intercession that does not spare itself

schools.summary: Gill: the plague had begun at one end of the camp and proceeded; Aaron placed himself between the part already smitten and the part not yet reached — a type of Christ, the Mediator between God and man, the living God and dead sinners, who by his atonement stays the wrath (1 Tim. 2:5).

themes: he stood between the dead and the living; and the plague was stayed
a type of Christ the Mediator
the atonement turning the wrath and dividing doomed from saved (1 Tim. 2:5)

schools.summary: Gill: thus what was threatened, that their carcasses should fall in the wilderness, was more and more fulfilled. Calvin: some three hundred had already perished with Korah, and now a far greater number was added — what the wicked reap from their obstinacy.

themes: they that died in the plague were fourteen thousand and seven hundred, beside them that died about... Korah
the carcasses falling as threatened
the grim harvest of obstinate rebellion

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron returned after he had by his atonement and intercession put a stop to the wrath; the plague was stayed, here repeated for certainty and to show it broke not out again.

themes: Aaron returned unto Moses... and the plague was stayed
the triumph of atonement and intercession
the God who smites, entreated, stays his hand
